content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcel PnecrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5ffi o Fax (916) 322-0886
November 4,2004
Linda Hill
San Bernardino City
Unified School District
777 North F Street
San Bernardino, CA 92410
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-218
Dear Ms. Hill:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the San Bernardino City
Unified School Diskict for advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of the political
Reform Act (the'Act').r
QUESTION
Is it permissible for school board members to be featured in high school
yearbooks?
CONCLUSION
Inclusion ofphotographs of elected school board members in yearbooks would
not violate the Act's mass mailing provisions, provided the bomd members do not request
or induce students to purchase the yearbooks.
rACTS
You seek advice as to whether it is permissible to print school bomd members,
photographs in high school yearbooks. The yearbooks are partially fundod by the school
district since the yearbook class is part of the students'regular schedule and is taught by a
district teacher. Approximately 525 books are sold to students at a cost of $75 each.
I Government Code sectiors 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
iqgjt


File No. A-04-218
Page No. 2
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 provides that "[n]o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
at public expense." A mass mailing is more than 200 substantially similar items sent in a
calendar month that features an elected officer affiliated with the agerrcy and any of the
costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50 are paid with public moneys.
(Regulation I 8901 (a).)
The mass mailing restrictions do not apply to materials that are sent in response to
an unsolicited request. An unsolicited request is "[a] written ororal communication
(including a petition) which specifically requests a req)onse and which is not requested or
induced by the recipient elected officed'orhis orher agent. (Regulation 18901(;X4XA).)
In your letter you stated that the yearbook is partially funded bypublic fgnds in
that the yearbook production is a regular class attended by studemts and Lught by a
salaried instructor- It is anticipated that 525 yearbooks will b€ produced. ft " yiarbook
meets the definition of a mass mailing unless an exception applies.
You indicated that the yearbook is sold to students at a cost of $75. In Harper
Advice Letter, No. I-89435, we said that whem students purchase yearbooks in reqronse
to solicitations by the students producing the yearbooks, such prrchases are unsolicited
requests, provided that the students who produce the yearbooks are not acting at the
behest of the school board members. Accordingly, provided your school Uoara does not
request or induce sfudents to purchase yearbooks, the yearbooks meet the test of an
unsolicitd request and would not be a mass mailing under the Act.
I trust this answers your question sufficiently- If you have any additional
questions, or need to discuss this firther, please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
Jeanette E. Turvill
Politi cal Reforrr Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
tET/jt

QUESTION: Is it permissible for school board members to be featured in high school
yearbooks?

CONCLUSION: Inclusion ofphotographs of elected school board members in yearbooks would
not violate the Act's mass mailing provisions, provided the bomd members do not request
or induce students to purchase the yearbooks.
